title: Polyvagal Theory and the Three States You Didn't Know You Were Living In
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: Polyvagal Theory offers a new map for understanding the three states of your nervous system. Learn how to move from survival mode to a felt sense of safety.
imageDescription: A luminous, abstract image of the human nervous system rendered as golden, glowing circuitry. The light emanates from a central point, representing the vagus nerve, and spreads outwards in warm, healing pathways against a soft, out-of-focus background.
htmlContent:
Nobody warns you that healing complex trauma means grieving a childhood that never existed, and that the body has been keeping a score it was never taught how to read. We are told to think positively, to talk about our feelings, to forgive the past, but the body operates on a much older and more primal logic - a logic of sensation, of threat, and of survival. This article is about learning to listen to that language, not as a problem to be solved, but as a significant source of wisdom and a pathway back to wholeness.

The Architecture of Survival: Beyond Fight or Flight

We have been handed a genuinely incomplete map of our own inner terrain, a map that speaks of fight or flight as if those are the only two territories of response available when threat appears on the horizon. This binary view is a disservice to the complexity of our own biology. But the reality of our lived, breathed, moment-to-moment experience is infinitely more layered, a sophisticated internal dance choreographed by the autonomic nervous system long before the conscious mind ever gets the memo. Polyvagal Theory, the work of Dr. These states are not choices but physiological realities, the legacy of an evolutionary road that prioritized survival above all else. Understanding this architecture is not an academic exercise; it is the beginning of learning to work with the very wiring that so often feels like it is working against us. It’s the difference between fighting the current and learning to navigate the river. It is about realizing that the nervous system is not a simple switch, but a complex, hierarchical system that is constantly assessing risk and safety in the environment through a process Porges calls 'neuroception'.

Ventral Vagal: The Harbor of Safe Connection

Sympathetic Activation: The Engine of Mobilization

Dorsal Vagal: The Stillness of the Freeze

The Social Engagement System: The Missing Piece

Beyond the three primary states of the polyvagal ladder lies a crucial component that is often overlooked in discussions of trauma and healing: the social engagement system. It turns out to be the system that allows us to connect with others, to feel safe in their presence, and to co-regulate our nervous systems. It is the system that is responsible for the subtle nuances of human interaction - the prosody of our voice, the expression on our face, the gesture of our hands. For those who have experienced complex trauma, this system is often deeply compromised. The very people who were supposed to be a source of safety and connection were instead a source of threat and danger. As a result, the social engagement system becomes wired for danger, constantly scanning for signs of threat and betrayal. The work of healing, then, is not just about regulating our own nervous system, but about re-patterning our social engagement system. It is about learning to distinguish between safe and unsafe people, to set boundaries, and to risk connection in small, manageable doses. It is about finding those rare and precious relationships where we can feel seen, heard, and valued for who we are, and allowing those experiences to slowly and gently rewire our capacity for love and belonging. This is not a quick fix, but a long and winding road that requires courage, patience, and a willingness to be vulnerable. But it is a road that leads back to the heart of what it means to be human: to love and be loved.

For a deeper exploration of these themes, see Kalesh's work on consciousness and healing.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

The Slow Road Back to Embodiment

Navigating these polyvagal states is not a matter of choosing the “good” one and avoiding the “bad” ones. Each state is a necessary and intelligent part of our survival repertoire. The work of healing is about increasing our capacity to move between these states with more flexibility and awareness. It is about becoming a student of our own nervous system, learning to read its signals not as pathologies but as communications. The pioneering work of researchers like Peter Levine has shown us that trauma is not in the event itself, but in the nervous system’s response to the event, and that the key to healing lies in completing those thwarted survival responses. What emerges is the essence of somatic work ~ allowing the body to finally do what it needed to do then, in a context of safety now. It is not about getting rid of our trauma responses, but about expanding our window of tolerance so that we are no longer held captive by them. It’s about understanding that safety is not a place, but a state of being. This process is not about erasing the past, but about changing our relationship to it. It is about learning to be with the sensations of the body without being overwhelmed by them, to witness the stories of the mind without being identified with them, and to access the inherent wisdom of the nervous system that is always striving for balance and wholeness. It is a slow and patient process of re-negotiating the terms of our own survival, of teaching the body that the war is over, and that it is finally safe to come home.

The Body as the Unspoken Truth

The mind creates intricate stories, justifications, and rationalizations to make sense of the nonsensical. It will build entire philosophies on a foundation of unprocessed trauma, mistaking intellectual understanding for embodied integration. But the body does not speak in narratives; it speaks in the language of sensation, of tension, of collapse, of breath held and released. It is the ultimate arbiter of truth, the living archive of our entire history. We can spend years in talk therapy, dissecting every memory and analyzing every pattern, and still find ourselves caught in the same cycles of activation and shutdown. That becomes because the trauma does not live in the story; it lives in the body. The work, then, is to learn to listen at a deeper level, beneath the noise of the thinking mind. This is not easy work, for the body holds the very feelings we have organized our lives around not feeling. But it is the only work that leads to a genuine and lasting change. It is the path from a life defined by the echoes of the past to one lived in the vibrant, ever-present reality of the now. For those seeking to deepen their understanding of consciousness and healing, the teachings of Kalesh offer a powerful resource for this process. This is the practice of embodiment, of moving from a life lived in the head to a life lived in the body. It is the practice of feeling our feet on the ground, of noticing the rhythm of our breath, of sensing the aliveness in our hands. It is a practice of coming home to ourselves, to the only place we have ever truly been.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between the dorsal vagal and ventral vagal states?

The ventral vagal state is a state of safety, connection, and social engagement, where we feel calm and present. The dorsal vagal state, on the other hand, is a state of shutdown or collapse, an immobilization response to overwhelming threat, often experienced as numbness or dissociation.

Can I be in more than one state at a time?

While one state is typically dominant, it is possible to experience a blending of states. For example, one might experience a mix of sympathetic activation and dorsal vagal shutdown, a state often described as a "functional freeze," where one is going through the motions of life without any real sense of connection or vitality.

For calming an overactive nervous system, you could also try Pure Encapsulations GABA (paid link).

Here is the thing, though.

How can I know which state I'm in?

The key is to cultivate interoception, the ability to sense the internal state of your body. This can be done through practices like mindfulness, body scanning, and gentle somatic inquiry. Over time, you can learn to recognize the unique signatures of each state in your own system - the heart rate, the muscle tension, the quality of your breath.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Nature Made Magnesium Glycinate 400mg (paid link).

Is the goal to always be in the ventral vagal state?

The goal is not to live exclusively in the ventral vagal state, but to have a flexible and resilient nervous system that can move through all the states appropriately and return to a baseline of safety. The sympathetic and dorsal vagal states are essential for survival; the problem is not the states themselves, but getting stuck in them.

The work through the field of the nervous system is not about arriving at a final destination of perpetual calm, but about becoming a skilled and compassionate navigator of your own inner world. It is about understanding that the very mechanisms that have caused so much pain are also the keys to a genuine and unshakable sense of wholeness. The question is not whether you can be fixed, but whether you are willing to meet the unfixed parts of yourself with a radical and unwavering presence. What if the goal is not to be healed, but to be whole? What if the path to wholeness is not through fixing, but through feeling? What if the very thing you have been running from is the doorway to the freedom you have been seeking? The body is not a problem to be solved, but a mystery to be explored. And in that exploration, we may just find the peace we have been searching for all along. The question is not whether you are broken, but whether you are willing to finally come home to yourself.
